sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 72, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current knowledge ledger from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE LEDGER.
Pass it as the `world_model` argument of the `python` tool call, alongside `code`. That argument is the one that carries forward, so send it on every call, even when you reply with a tool call and no assistant text.
Game-tier fields (action_semantics, object_taxonomy, hud_map, win_pattern, level_log, cross_level_notes) persist across levels; level-tier fields (world_model, goal_model, recent_findings, open_questions, current_plan, strategy_log, failed_probes) reset when a level ends. Record every unproductive probe in failed_probes so it is never repeated, and tag claims VERIFIED(step N) or HYPOTHESIS.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Knowledge ledger carried from earlier turns:
Game knowledge (persists across levels):
- Action semantics: {'RIGHT': 'Moves avatar +3 cols right, charcoal moves -3 cols (opposite) (VERIFIED steps 1-4,56)', 'LEFT': 'Moves avatar -3 cols left, charcoal moves +3 cols (opposite) (VERIFIED steps 5-6,58-61)', 'DOWN': 'Moves avatar +3 rows down, charcoal moves -3 rows (opposite) (VERIFIED steps 7-10)', 'UP': 'Moves avatar -3 rows up, charcoal moves +3 rows (opposite) (VERIFIED steps 11-13)', 'SPACE': 'Moves avatar UP 3 rows only in specific conditions (step 51), no effect otherwise (steps 46,53,55)', 'MOUSE': 'No effect (VERIFIED step 43)', 'ACTION7': 'No effect (VERIFIED step 42)'}
- Object taxonomy: {'black_with_dots': 'Player avatar - black shape with 5 white dots, moves with arrow keys by 3 cells', 'charcoal_shape': 'Charcoal object that moves OPPOSITE to avatar by 3 cells', 'yellow_shape': 'Target - yellow L-shape, moves opposite to avatar, grows when avatar approaches (18<->27 pixels)', 'sky_blue_strip': 'Vertical divider at cols 30-32', 'right_border_B': 'Black strip at top-right, can merge with avatar when RIGHT pressed near border', 'yellow_border_strip': 'Yellow strip at col 63, shrinks over time (19->11 pixels)'}
- HUD map: {'bottom_row': 'Black strip at row 63', 'right_col': 'Yellow strip at col 63 - shrinking (possibly timer or progress)'}
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it. Tag claims VERIFIED(step N) when the evidence is in `history`, otherwise HYPOTHESIS.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
The `world_model` tool argument is the reliable way to update the ledger. When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 71 actions; a strong score needs about 32 or fewer. You are 2.2x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The game is now back to NOT_FINISHED state after RIGHT. The avatar and charcoal are now adjacent on the left side of the divider. Looking at the image, the avatar (black with dots) and charcoal (dark gray) are now next to each other, separated only by the sky blue divider.
Wait - looking more carefully at the image, the avatar is on the left of the divider and the charcoal is on the right of the divider. They're very close to each other. This is a new configuration.
Key observations:
1. After game over, the game reset to a new starting position (avatar at top-left, charcoal at top-right of divider)
2. RIGHT moved the avatar right by 3, bringing it closer to the divider
3. The game is now NOT_FINISHED again
The game over was triggered by DOWN. This suggests that moving DOWN in certain positions causes game over. The goal might be to avoid game over and achieve something else.
Looking at the current state:
- Avatar is near the divider on the left side
- Charcoal is near the divider on the right side
- Target is at bottom-right
I need to understand what the actual goal is. The game over happened when I moved DOWN from a position where the avatar was on the left side. Maybe the goal is to get the avatar to a specific position without triggering game over?
Let me think about what I know:
- The yellow border strip shrinks over time (possibly a timer)
- DOWN caused game over
- The avatar and charcoal move in opposite directions
- The target also moves opposite to avatar
Maybe the goal is to get the avatar to the same side as the target (right side) without triggering game over? Or maybe the goal is to position the avatar and charcoal in a specific configuration?
Let me try moving RIGHT again to cross the divider and see what happens. But wait, I already crossed the divider before (to the left side) and that didn't complete the level. 
Actually, looking at the image more carefully - the avatar is now on the LEFT side of the divider and the charcoal is on the RIGHT side. This is the opposite of before! Before, the avatar was on the right and charcoal on the left.
Let me try to understand the game better. Maybe the goal is to get the avatar and target on the same side, or to get the avatar to overlap with the target somehow.
Let me try moving RIGHT to cross back to the right side and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Game recovered from game over. Avatar and charcoal now adjacent across divider.
# Let me check current positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['pixels'] > 10:
        b = n['boundary']
        r, c = min([p[0] for p in b]), min([p[1] for p in b])
        print(f"  {n['color']}: top=({r},{c}), pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Avatar is near divider on left, charcoal on right
# Target is at bottom-right
# Need to figure out win condition
# Hypothesis: maybe need to get avatar to overlap with target on right side
# Try RIGHT to cross divider
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
<parameter=world_model>

Game is NOT_FINISHED again.", "goal_model": "Unknown - need to avoid game over and find win condition", "recent_findings": ["Game over can be recovered from", "Avatar and charcoal swap sides after game over"], "open_questions": ["What is the win condition?"], "current_plan": "Try crossing divider to right side", "strategy_log": [], "failed_probes": ["ACTION7", "MOUSE", "DOWN caused game over"]}}
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
